issue_title: JSON generation bug for neighbor-changes
HTTP API 'show routes' capture
Attributes appearing on withdraws
issue_body:
Hi,

After running for a while with lots of incoming updates (from a full-table feed), we start to see withdraw update messages which have attributes on them, despite the underlying message not having them (normally a withdraw just contains a list of prefixes unless there is also an announce in the same message)

We see the following example output:
```
{ "exabgp": "3.4.6", "time": 1432043305.52, "host" : "rrc-test.ripe.net", "pid" : "6388", "ppid" : "1", "type": "update", "header": "FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF001F02", "body": "0008156D46A0145113700000", "neighbor": { "ip": "77.72.229.74", "address": { "local": "193.0.19.22", "peer": "77.72.229.74"}, "asn": { "local": "65000", "peer": "12654"}, "message": { "update": { "attribute": { "origin": "igp", "as-path": [ 12654, 9002, 57023, 36947, 36947 ], "confederation-path": [], "atomic-aggregate": false }, "withdraw": { "ipv4 unicast": { "109.70.160.0/21": {  }, "81.19.112.0/20": {  } } } } }} }
```

You can see there is an as-path, origin, etc on a withdraw. Yet, if I take the same 'header' and 'body', and put it back into exabgp --decode:
```
/opt/exabgp/bin/python2.6 /opt/exabgp/lib/python2.6/site-packages/exabgp/application/bgp.py --decode FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF001F020008156D46A0145113700000 /opt/exabgp/etc/exabgp/exabgp_instance_6.conf`
<snip>
Tue, 19 May 2015 14:40:43 | INFO     | 11368  | parser        | the message is an update
Tue, 19 May 2015 14:40:43 | INFO     | 11368  | parser        | no announced NLRI
Tue, 19 May 2015 14:40:43 | INFO     | 11368  | parser        | parsing 1/1 nlri payload 156D 46A0 1451 1370
Tue, 19 May 2015 14:40:43 | INFO     | 11368  | parser        | parsed withdraw nlri 109.70.160.0/21 next-hop none payload 156D 46A0
Tue, 19 May 2015 14:40:43 | INFO     | 11368  | parser        | parsing 1/1 nlri payload 1451 1370
Tue, 19 May 2015 14:40:43 | INFO     | 11368  | parser        | parsed withdraw nlri 81.19.112.0/20 next-hop none payload 1451 1370
Tue, 19 May 2015 14:40:43 | INFO     | 11368  | parser        |
Tue, 19 May 2015 14:40:43 | INFO     | 11368  | parser        | decoded update withdrawn 109.70.160.0/21 next-hop none
Tue, 19 May 2015 14:40:43 | INFO     | 11368  | parser        | decoded update withdrawn 81.19.112.0/20 next-hop none
Tue, 19 May 2015 14:32:45 | INFO     | 10871  | parser        | update json { "exabgp": "3.4.0", "time": 1432045965, "host" : "rrc-test.ripe.net", "pid" : "10871", "ppid" : "6144", "type": "update", "neighbor": { "ip": "77.72.229.74", "address": { "local": "193.0.19.22", "peer": "77.72.229.74"}, "asn": { "local": "65000", "peer": "12654"}, "message": { "update": { "withdraw": { "ipv4 unicast": { "109.70.160.0/21": {  }, "81.19.112.0/20": {  } } } } }} }
```


(This is for version 3.4.6 btw)

The same message on the wire is decoded by TShark as follows:
```
Border Gateway Protocol - UPDATE Message
    Marker: ffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffff
    Length: 31
    Type: UPDATE Message (2)
    Unfeasible routes length: 8 bytes
    Withdrawn routes:
        109.70.160.0/21
            Withdrawn route prefix length: 21
            Withdrawn prefix: 109.70.160.0 (109.70.160.0)
        81.19.112.0/20
            Withdrawn route prefix length: 20
            Withdrawn prefix: 81.19.112.0 (81.19.112.0)
    Total path attribute length: 0 bytes
```

I'd guess it is an attribute caching issue - we haven't managed to capture this happening where we have both an announce and a subsequent withdraw to compare the attributes. Also, it doesn't happen all the time!

If you need any extra info, please let me know

Thanks,
Colin

issue_title: Review each driver for obsolete attributes and other things that should not be public
issue_body:
Any that are already obsolete before first release should be marked as don't code gen.
There may be other things we don't want to expose:

* Things from IVI spec that don't make sense in Python
* P2P related things
* NI-TClk related things
* Properties for obsolete / EOL hardware
* Things only useful with complex data
* Calibration 
* Other internal things

Some of these may become public later, but we don't want to prematurely expose them.

Will need to work with driver teams to review.

The doc recommends docker as the fastest way to get started and says that:

`$ docker run -p 8000:8000 -it --rm allennlp/allennlp`

will download the latest allennlp image, start a Docker container, and launch an interactive shell. It also exposes port 8000, which is where the demo server runs, and shuts down the container when you exit the interactive shell.

However, all it did was to bring me to a root prompt and when attempting to execute the allennlp command, it is unavailable. 
`bash: allennlp: command not found`  

After a little looking around, I figured that I had to execute `python -m allennlp.run` to get anything to happen.

Am I missing/overlooking something?

issue_title: Postgres password required in diamond config file
issue_body:
The postgres collector has a `postgres` default string for the password connection. This basically forces to override the password by writing it in clear text in the `diamond.conf` file or to use a weak auth mode such as  `trust` in `pg_hba.conf`.

There should be a way to not pass a password to the psycopg2 `connect()` function so that the password can be specified in the `.pgpass` file.

issue_title: question: how to start cadvisor again?
issue_body:
Hi,

Newbie here. As you've seen in the previous issue ;).

I'm testing the platform, randomly resetting machines and seeing if I can manage to nurse the clusters back to health again. It seems to be doing alright, but of course, there's no load on the platform.

Anyway, as a part of trying to get some diagnostics into each of the services running in docker, I installed cadvisor according [to the docs](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/platform_monitoring/#cadvisor) with a unit file that I did `fleetctl start cadvisor.service`, which started it across all three nodes that I have in my vagrant cluster.

I then reset a single node, node 3 and watched whether things would still show up and route properly. They did.

Now I started it again.

Here's where the first question comes: if a node reboots, why doesn't `deisctl status` tell me about it? It's just blank, exiting 0.

Also, when the node goes back up, not all unit are automatically started, even though they should optimally be started as soon as possible to ensure availability: specifically:

deis-store-daemon.service		169914f2.../172.17.8.102	activating	start-pre

and 

deis-logspout.service			169914f2.../172.17.8.102	activating	auto-restart

I had to run `deisctl start platform` for them to start. I didn't manage any `fleetctl` command that made a difference. At this point it was a bit opaque.

When they had started, I noticed that cadvisor looked like this:

```
UNIT					MACHINE				ACTIVE		SUB
cadvisor.service			169914f2.../172.17.8.102	failed		failed
cadvisor.service			21d1548b.../172.17.8.100	active		running
cadvisor.service			28ed8692.../172.17.8.101	active		running
deis-builder.service			21d1548b.../172.17.8.100	active		running
# snip
```

As it turns out, it didn't want to start again, because, according to journalctl (deisctl journal didn't want to give me anything:

```
$ deisctl journal cadvisor
Unable to get journal for global unit cadvisor.service. Check the logs on the host using journalctl.
```
) despite it being a global unit.

```
$ journalctl -fu cadvisor
Mar 06 23:00:33 deis-03 systemd[1]: Starting Google Container Advisor...
Mar 06 23:00:33 deis-03 systemd[1]: Started Google Container Advisor.
Mar 06 23:00:33 deis-03 docker[3135]: time="2015-03-06T23:00:33Z" level="fatal" msg="Error response from daemon: Conflict, The name cadvisor is already assigned to 0dc97d3eb6b8. You have to delete (or rename) that container to be able to assign cadvisor to a container again."
Mar 06 23:00:33 deis-03 systemd[1]: cadvisor.service: main process exited, code=exited, status=1/FAILURE
Mar 06 23:00:33 deis-03 systemd[1]: Unit cadvisor.service entered failed state.
Mar 06 23:00:33 deis-03 systemd[1]: cadvisor.service failed.
```

How to start cadvisor again? Do I have to manually go through and SSH to all machines to `docker rm -f cadvisor` the containers and their images?

And why don't needed containers, such as the ceph monitor container, start when the node goes back up? I'm absolutely certain a restart will bite me in the ass at some point, me failing to notice it and then another node crashing, pulling everything down with it. So I'd rather save my ass when I have time to learn how to do it. ;)

issue_title: Automate upgrade test
issue_body:
We'd like to add the ability test the deis upgrade process in an automated fashion. This process currently is:

- spin up the previous release of deis
- deploy an app to it
- verify that the app is running properly (smoke test)
- tear down deis around the app
- deploy the current version of deis
- verify that the app is running properly (smoke test)

Ideally this test should be able to run on all providers, but for the first round of implementation I'm only looking to support Vagrant and EC2.

issue_title: Affinity arg is not working correctly
issue_body:
Right now if you set the affinity arg, I would expect that if a url does not contain that argument at all, it will be routed with no affinity.  But it appears that instead it is routed consistently to the same server, as if it were consistently hashing the undefined value.

This is a problem for us, because we only want to use sticky routing on one of our services (for xhr polling / sockets), but the rest we would like to be load balanced in the standard way.

issue_title: Gas price engine API
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

Currently, web3.py just uses the `eth_gasPrice` JSON-RPC API to determine the gas price to use for transactions.  Most nodes do not currently implement very good algorithms for determining gas prices.

### How can it be fixed?

Lets implement a new API for determining gas prices.

* Add a new API: `Web3.set_gas_price_engine(engine)`

The `engine` will be a callable with the function signature:

```
engine(w3, transaction_params)
```

* `w3` will be the `Web3` instance
* `transaction_params` will be a dictionary of the transaction params being sent.

The callable **must** return a positive integer which represents the `gas_price` in the *wei* denomination.

The **default** engine that a web3 instance should use is one that simply returns `web3.eth.gasPrice`

issue_title: Setting proxy configuration from command line
issue_body:
Is it possible to set proxy configuration from command line? `conda config` only seems to support setting primitive (via `--set`) or sequence (via `--add`/`--append`/`--prepend`) parameters, but `proxy_servers` parameter is a map, so attemping to use `--set` or `--add` just throws an error.

As a result it just doesn't seem possible to update the config programmatically. Writing or appending to the `.condarc` file directly is not really an option because it may conflict with existing user's configuration.

I want something like the following to work:

```bash
$ conda config --add proxy_servers.http http://proxy.tld
$ conda config --add proxy_servers.https https://proxy.tld
```

issue_title: `Dead Kernel` happens everytime I try to run a command (OSX)
issue_body: I get this error: ``` [I 14:48:47.243 NotebookApp] Kernel started: ced3c457-52f1-4d0e-a2f4-5183dfc066ff [1] "Got unhandled msg_type:" "comm_open" Error in unlockBinding("base_display", displayenv) : no binding for "base_display" Calls: ... -> handle_shell -> -> unlockBinding Execution halted [I 14:48:50.246 NotebookApp] KernelRestarter: restarting kernel (1/5) WARNING:root:kernel ced3c457-52f1-4d0e-a2f4-5183dfc066ff restarted ``` Here's some specs: ``` $ which R /Users/jespinoz/anaconda/bin/R ``` from command line ``` R version 3.3.1 (2016-06-21) -- "Bug in Your Hair" Copyright (C) 2016 The R Foundation for Statistical Computing Platform: x86_64-apple-darwin11.0.0 (64-bit) ``` Jupyter version ``` $ jupyter --version 4.1.0 ``` conda version ``` $ conda --version conda 4.1.11 ``` IRKernel ``` $ conda update r-irkernel Using Anaconda Cloud api site https://api.anaconda.org Fetching package metadata ........... # All requested packages already installed. # packages in environment at /Users/jespinoz/anaconda: # r-irkernel 0.6.1 r3.3.1_0 r jespinozlt-osx:~ jespinoz$ ``` IRDisplay ``` $ conda update r-irdisplay Using Anaconda Cloud api site https://api.anaconda.org Fetching package metadata ........... # All requested packages already installed. # packages in environment at /Users/jespinoz/anaconda: # r-irdisplay 0.3 r3.3.1_2 r ``` Originally here: https://github.com/IRkernel/IRkernel/issues/396 but on https://github.com/IRkernel/IRkernel/issues/204#issuecomment-240692343 they told me to migrate it to conda.

issue_title: [Windows] Activated environment is not working properly when calling python scripts from command line
issue_body:
I discovered this issue when creating a separate environment for my django installation.

I created a new environment called "django", activated it and installed pip and then django. Next, when I enter `django-admin.py startproject mysite` (cf. django tutorial), the django-admin.py script fails because it cannot find the django modules in my environment. After some troubleshooting, I discoverd that  the problem was the Python *sys.path* variable. 

The *sys.path* available to the script did not contain references to the site-packages directory of the "django" environment, but instead contained references to the site-packages directory of the main environment. The only reference to the django environment is the Scripts directory of the environment. What happens is that when django-admin.py is called, the python.exe interpreter from the main environment is used, instead of the one in the "django" environment. 

Apparently, sys.path uses the location of the python interpreter to determine the site-packages directory. When I used the command `ftype Python.File=c:\Anaconda\envs\django\python.exe "%1" %*`, the correct python interpreter is used, *sys.path* refers to the site-packages directory of the django environment, and the django-admin script finds the django modules. 

Therefore I suggest that the activate and deactivate scripts are updated to make sure that the correct Python interpreter is used. On windows 8 this requires the ftype command, which however requires administrator rights.

issue_title: Specify a channel in which to search for dependencies during build
issue_body:
I am trying to integrate travis-ci with a [project on which I'm working](http://github.com/jrsmith3/ibei/). Travis-ci is failing at the build step of the package with the [error](https://travis-ci.org/jrsmith3/ibei/jobs/55853116#L684)

```
$ conda build -c jrsmith3 conda.recipe
There was an error importing jinja2.
Please run `conda install jinja2` to enable jinja template support
Removing old build directory
Removing old work directory
BUILD START: ibei--np19py27_0
Fetching package metadata: ........
Error: No packages found in current linux-64 channels matching: physicalproperty
The command "conda build -c jrsmith3 conda.recipe" failed and exited with 1 during .
Your build has been stopped.
```

I think the problem is that one of the dependencies of the package under test is only available from my binstar channel. As you can see in the snippet above, I'm calling the build command with the "-c jrsmith3" flag, so I think `conda` should be searching my binstar channel for dependencies.

I don't understand why travis-ci can't build my package. Is there anything I am missing?


[`.travis.yml`](https://github.com/jrsmith3/ibei/blob/cd68b9d47c1770cc9840dafae9618b25b9d1c41b/.travis.yml):

```yaml
language: python
python:
  # We don't actually use the Travis Python, but this keeps it organized.
  - "2.6"
  - "2.7"
  - "3.3"
  - "3.4"
install:
  - sudo apt-get update
  # You may want to periodically update this, although the conda update
  # conda line below will keep everything up-to-date.  We do this
  # conditionally because it saves us some downloading if the version is
  # the same.
  - if [[ "$TRAVIS_PYTHON_VERSION" == "2.7" ]]; then
      wget http://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda-3.4.2-Linux-x86_64.sh -O miniconda.sh;
    else
      wget http://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda3-3.4.2-Linux-x86_64.sh -O miniconda.sh;
    fi
  - bash miniconda.sh -b -p $HOME/miniconda
  - export PATH="$HOME/miniconda/bin:$PATH"
  - hash -r
  - conda config --set always_yes yes --set changeps1 no
  - conda update -q conda
  - conda install conda-build
  # Useful for debugging any issues with conda
  - conda info -a

  # Replace dep1 dep2 ... with your dependencies
  - conda create -q -n test-environment python=$TRAVIS_PYTHON_VERSION anaconda
  - source activate test-environment
  - conda build -c jrsmith3 conda.recipe
  - conda install ibei --use-local

script: nosetests
```


The package's [`meta.yaml`](https://github.com/jrsmith3/ibei/blob/cd68b9d47c1770cc9840dafae9618b25b9d1c41b/conda.recipe/meta.yaml):

```yaml
package:
    name: ibei
    # version: x.y.z
    # The version string is stored in `ibei/version.py`.
    # `build.sh` includes code to properly incorporate the version
    # string in the built package.

source:
    path: ..

requirements:
    build:
        - python
        - numpy
        - astropy
        - sympy
        - physicalproperty
    run:
        - python
        - numpy
        - astropy
        - sympy
        - physicalproperty

test:
    files:
        - ../test/test_DeVosSolarcell.py
        - ../test/test_SBSolarcell.py
        - ../test/test_uiebi.py
    requires:
        - nose

about:
    home: https://github.com/jrsmith3/ibei
    license: MIT
    summary: Calculator for incomplete Bose-Einstein integral
```

issue_title: Conda installs dependencies from the wrong channel
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior I'm using conda 4.5.0 with two channels, like this (`~/.condarc`): ``` channels: - defaults - conda-forge ``` so I expect packages to be pulled from `defaults`, if available, or from `conda-forge` otherwise. It happens that try to update a package from `conda-force` for which there is actually no update available, some dependencies are still pulled from `conda-forge` for no apparent reason. ### Steps to Reproduce I explicitly installed a package from `conda-forge`, like this: ``` conda install gmt -c conda-forge/label/dev ``` I don't think this package is getting updated automatically with `conda update --all` (as newers versions are not in the `defaults` channel), so I manually gave the install command once again, to force an update: ``` conda install gmt -c conda-forge/label/dev ``` Turns out that no new version is available, but conda still tries to install two packages, replacing the currently installed version (from `defaults`) with versions from `conda-forge`: ``` Solving environment: done ## Package Plan ## environment location: /home/pl/miniconda3/envs/sismo added / updated specs: - gmt The following packages will be downloaded: package | build ---------------------------|----------------- numpy-1.14.0rc1 |py36_blas_openblas_200 9.0 MB conda-forge/label/dev scipy-1.0.0 |py36_blas_openblas_201 40.3 MB conda-forge ------------------------------------------------------------ Total: 49.3 MB The following packages will be UPDATED: scipy: 1.0.0-py36hbf646e7_0 --> 1.0.0-py36_blas_openblas_201 conda-forge [blas_openblas] The following packages will be DOWNGRADED: numpy: 1.14.2-py36hdbf6ddf_0 --> 1.14.0rc1-py36_blas_openblas_200 conda-forge/label/dev [blas_openblas] Proceed ([y]/n)? ``` ## Expected Behavior No package is installed/updated/downgraded. ## Environment Information

issue_title: Help of miniconda windows installer is missing doc for silent install mode
issue_body:
Hi,

I was using the Miniconda windows installer from http://conda.pydata.org/miniconda.html.

A windows with the following text shows up, when running `Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe /?`:
```
Usage Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe [options]
Options:

/InstallationType=AllUsers [default: JustMe]

/AddToPath=[0|1] [default: 1]

/RegisterPython=[0|1] [default: AllUsers: 1, JustMe: 0]

Examples:
Install for all users, but don't add to PATH env var:
Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe /InstallationType=AllUsers
/AddToPath=0

Install for just me, but register the system Python:
Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe /RegisterPython=1
```

This option list is missing the already! available options
```
/S

/D=C:\Miniconda
```
for silent install mode (unattended install mode) and the installation directory respectively.

Also the documentation on http://conda.pydata.org/miniconda.html, or http://conda.pydata.org/docs/index.html are not mentioning the command line options of the installer for conda / e.g. miniconda.

The only hint I could find was: http://docs.continuum.io/anaconda/install.html#windows-install.

issue_title: conda needs to be more forgiving of bad channel responses
issue_body:
While investigating the cause of #1036 I looked into the `.condarc` file which specifies:

```
channels:
  - https://conda.anaconda.org/t/an-c89909f5-b1ad-4c73-9500-9e5f0e710c33/anaconda-nb-extensions
  - http://localhost:8080/conda/anaconda
  - defaults
```

The second entry is for a local deployment of Anaconda Repository.  It turns out the license had expired, so the responses conda got from that channel were bogus.  That is mentioned in more than one place, but at least one of them is https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1189.

Because Anaconda Repository did not return the JSON result that conda was expecting, conda then threw this stack trace:

```
$ sudo -u wakari /opt/wakari/miniconda/bin/conda create -p /tmp/testenv python
Fetching package metadata: ....An unexpected error has occurred, please consider sending the
following traceback to the conda GitHub issue tracker at:
  
    https://github.com/conda/conda/issues

Include the output of the command 'conda info' in your report.


Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/bin/conda", line 5, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/main.py", line 195, in main
    args_func(args, p)
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/main.py", line 202, in args_func
    args.func(args, p)
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/main_create.py", line 49, in execute
    install.install(args, parser, 'create')
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/install.py", line 239, in install
    offline=args.offline)
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/common.py", line 597, in get_index_trap
    return get_index(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/api.py", line 42, in get_index
    unknown=unknown)
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/utils.py", line 118, in __call__
    value = self.func(*args, **kw)
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/fetch.py", line 264, in fetch_index
    reversed(channel_urls))
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/fetch.py", line 263, in <lambda>
    use_cache=use_cache, session=session)),
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/fetch.py", line 65, in func
    res = f(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/opt/wakari/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/fetch.py", line 103, in fetch_repodata
    cache = json.loads(bz2.decompress(resp.content).decode('utf-8'))
IOError: invalid data stream
```

Because this kind of scenario is possible for several reasons I believe conda needs to be made more robust against getting back bogus JSON.

Of course Anaconda Repository should also be more helpful and not pretend to return `repodata.json` (which I think is happening here), so I will also submit an issue there.

issue_title: Anaconda Python + pyodbc does not work
issue_body:
Anaconda Python is not able to run a python script successfully that works with system python. 

To repro:
```
$pip install pyodbc
$sudo su 
$curl https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc | apt-key add -
$curl https://packages.microsoft.com/config/ubuntu/16.04/prod.list > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/mssql-release.list
$exit
$sudo apt-get update
$sudo ACCEPT_EULA=Y apt-get install msodbcsql=13.1.6.0-1 mssql-tools=14.0.3.0-1 unixodbc-dev
$pip install pyodbc
$vim test.py
```
Paste the following in test.py
```
import pyodbc
server = 'localhost'
database = 'SampleDB'
username = 'sa'
password = 'your_password'
cnxn = pyodbc.connect('DRIVER={ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server};SERVER='+server+';PORT=1433;DATABASE='+database+';UID='+username+';PWD='+ password)
cursor = cnxn.cursor()
print ('Reading data from table')
tsql = "SELECT @@VERSION;"
with cursor.execute(tsql):
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    while row:
        print (str(row[0]))
        row = cursor.fetchone()
```


```$ python test.py```
-- Works! 

```
$cd /usr/bin/anaconda/bin/
$./pip install pyodbc
$./python test.py
```
This gives an error:
`pyodbc.Error: ('01000', "[01000] [unixODBC][Driver Manager]Can't open lib '/opt/microsoft/msodbcsql/lib64/libmsodbcsql-13.1.so.6.0' : file not found (0) (SQLDriverConnect)")`
 
I think it is something about permissions (anaconda python not being able to read from certain folders)

Some other issues on StackOverflow:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/42301519/pyodbc-cannot-find-unixodbc-library-when-using-anaconda-python

issue_title: Can't use an unsigned integer array to index a table
issue_body:
An `IndexError` is raised when attempting to index a table using an array of unsigned integer data type.  See example below.  Since unsigned integers can always be safely converted to signed integers, this appears to be an oversight when doing the data type checking.

```python
import bcolz as bz
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
d = bz.ctable.fromdataframe(pd.DataFrame({'x':list(range(10))}))
d[np.array([2,1,3], dtype='uint32')]
```

issue_title: Installation on Python 3 misses Cython dependency (0.9.0 regression)
issue_body:
Since release 0.9.0, `pip install pysam` on Python 3 fails because Cython is missing:

```
$ virtualenv -p `which python3.5` py35
$ . py35/bin/activate
$ pip install pysam
Collecting pysam
  Using cached pysam-0.9.0.tar.gz
    Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
    checking for gcc... gcc
    checking whether the C compiler works... yes
...
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/tmp/pip-build-8096gdno/pysam/setup.py", line 290, in <module>
        .format(fn))
    ValueError: no cython installed, but can not find pysam/chtslib.c.Make sure that cython is installed when building from the repository
    
    ----------------------------------------
Command "python setup.py egg_info" failed with error code 1 in /tmp/pip-build-8096gdno/pysam
```

Release 0.8.4 was good.

I tested Python 3.3, 3.4, and 3.5, they all fail in the same way. Python 2.7 seems to be good though, I'm not sure why.

It looks like the `requires` argument to setup is ignored (it lists `cython (>=0.21)`). I have no idea what causes this, but there have been quite some changes to `setup.py` since 0.8.4.

issue_title: bus error response
issue_body:
God kväll

I have a rather generic point about the bus master implementation.
It is currently not possible to check bus error responses from the tb driver. The AXI4L master simply checks for an ok bresp or throws an error, the wishbone master simply does not support an err signal.

In my world view, a complete test set of a bus slave that might return an error would include a test that actually triggers the error and checks it.
For that, the bus master model must allow to read the bus response somehow.

Is there a specific reason that the bus master package does not allow this?
Or may I make a feature request to support this?

Best regards,
emanuel

issue_title: Delete old indices when disk space limit has been exceeded for all cluster
issue_body:
Hello,

As I can see, at the moment we have 2 options to perform index delete operation:
 - delete indices which are older than a number of days
 - delete indices which disk space allocation is more than a number of gigabytes

But there is no option to delete the oldest index when we actually ran out of disk space on the machines.

For example, consider a 2 nodes cluster with a 1 Tb disk space per node. Logstash creates new index every day. In a standart situation, it is ok to store indices for 30 days. But if the logs DEBUG mode was switched on for a couple of days, we can just ran out of space.

I think it might be helpful to have a `cluster-disc-space` parameter = 2 Tb which can be used to delete the oldest logstash indices when the space consumed across all nodes exceeded mentioned threshold.

Is it possible, or maybe I'm missing something?

issue_title: Individual PyPi packages are missing LICENSE.txt file
issue_body:
While packaging the Azure CLI utilities for openSUSE, we noticed that the individual packages on PyPi.org (e.g. ```azure-cli-acr```) are missing their LICENSE.txt files. Only the ```azure-cli``` package itself includes this file.

Since each PyPi package is packaged as an individual RPM distribution package in openSUSE and every RPM package needs to include a license or a copyright file, this would be considered a packaging bug.

To mitigate the issue in openSUSE, I have just copied the LICENSE.txt from the ```azure-cli``` package into the packaging sources of every individual component package. However, it would be great if this issue could be fixed upstream so that other distributions like Debian don't run into this particular issue either.

Since both ```azure-cli``` and the individual component packages on PyPi are generated from the same, single source tree, I would advise to just copy the LICENSE.txt into the source directory of each PyPi package before generating the tarball. This avoids redundant copies of the LICENSE.txt file in the original source tree for Azure CLI.

issue_title: az vm create `--image` validation incorrectly suggesting --use-unmanaged-disk
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**

`az vm create --image` is doing a poor job at validating and expressing need for `--use-unmanaged-disk`. If valid URI, it suppress the need for that flag, if invalid URI it suggests unmanaged disk is needed (where the real problem is invalid URI).

It seems  `az vm create --image` shouldn't validate whether `--use-unmanaged-disk` is required. It seems to default to that if unknown/invalid URI. Somehow it knows a valid URI so perhaps there is a missing check here somewhere.

**To Reproduce**
1.  `az vm create -n "blahblah" -g "blahrsg" --size "Standard_DS5_v2_Promo" --image "MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:latest"`

Confusing and _misleading_ error message (tells me I want unmanaged disks... no i don't):
```
invalid usage for storage profile: create unmanaged OS disk created from generalized VHD:
        missing: --os-type, --use-unmanaged-disk
```

2. Ok lets play along and try this:
`az vm create -n "blahblah" -g "blahrsg" --size "Standard_DS5_v2_Promo" --image "MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:latest" --use-unmanaged-disk --os-type "windows"`
This error message tells me what the first command should of told me:
```
Deployment failed. Correlation ID: 3225ef51-8598-46fa-98c8-f6cfaa969b61. {
  "error": {
    "code": "InvalidParameter",
    "target": "osDisk.image.uri",
    "message": "The URI microsoftwindowsdesktop:Windows-10:latest does not look to be correct blob URI."
  }
}
```

3. Lets identify the correct blob URI:
`az vm image list --offer Windows-10 --all --output table`
```
Offer       Publisher                Sku       Urn                                                       Version
----------  -----------------------  --------  --------------------------------------------------------  ------------
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  RS3-Pro   MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:RS3-Pro:16299.431.41   16299.431.41
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  RS3-Pro   MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:RS3-Pro:16299.492.43   16299.492.43
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  RS3-Pro   MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:RS3-Pro:16299.551.46   16299.551.46
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  RS3-ProN  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:RS3-ProN:16299.431.41  16299.431.41
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  RS3-ProN  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:RS3-ProN:16299.492.43  16299.492.43
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  RS3-ProN  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:RS3-ProN:16299.551.46  16299.551.46
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  rs4-pro   MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:rs4-pro:17134.112.43   17134.112.43
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  rs4-pro   MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:rs4-pro:17134.167.46   17134.167.46
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  rs4-pro   MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:rs4-pro:17134.48.41    17134.48.41
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  rs4-pron  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:rs4-pron:17134.112.43  17134.112.43
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  rs4-pron  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:rs4-pron:17134.167.46  17134.167.46
Windows-10  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop  rs4-pron  MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:rs4-pron:17134.48.41   17134.48.41
```

4. `az vm create -n "blahblah" -g "blahrsg" --size "Standard_DS5_v2_Promo" --image "MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:latest"`
```
{created VM}
```

**Expected behavior**
1.  `az vm create -n "blahblah" -g "blahrsg" --size "Standard_DS5_v2_Promo" --image "MicrosoftWindowsDesktop:Windows-10:latest"`

Informative error:
```
"message": "The URI microsoftwindowsdesktop:Windows-10:latest does not look to be correct blob URI."
```

**Environment summary**
Azure Shell - Bash.

**Additional context**
Why I am here today: Windows-10 SKU selection should not be so complex. 

The default image SKUs include Ubuntu, UbuntuLTS, WindowsServer2012 etc.... why not Windows10?

Ultimately what I really am looking for today was an easy way to spin a standard Window10 VM, without having to lookup specific URIs or other nonsense: `az vm create -n "blahblah" -g "blahrsg" --size "Standard_DS5_v2_Promo" --image "Windows10"`

issue_title: webapp: 'DOCKER_REGISTRY_SERVER_PASSWORD' should be write only
issue_body: For now, webapp service just treats it as a regular app-setting and exposes it on any read operation. This is not ideal due to security concerns. CLI makes workaround through #3656 to hide it. So opening this tracking issue so we will remove the workaround when the service side makes the improvement.

issue_title: Web docs don't display placeholders in angle brackets
issue_body: In examples, we sometimes have placeholders like \<this\>.  However, web markup swallows this entire thing, and the resulting web documentation appears like a blank.  Instead, we should use {this}, which appears just fine.

issue_title: No way to get CIFS URI for storage shares
issue_body: Mounting a storage share is a core scenario for using shares via the CLI, yet there is no way that I can see to retrieve the CIFS url for a given share, which is required to mount it.  I think is important not only for convenience, but to ensure that the share URI is retrieved in a reliable fashion that will work in all Azure clouds.

issue_title: Please do not recommend to run a bash script from a $random URL with sudo
issue_body:
currently you suggest (as first point on Readme.rst) to run the following command:

` $ curl -L https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCli | sudo bash`

At this point you recommend to the users to run a random script with administrative privileges without even verifying if the script is actually yours. From a security point of view this is a very bad idea. At least you could verify a signature before you let the user run this script.

issue_title: [ACR] Showing credentials fails with an unfriendly message when the admin account isn't enabled
issue_body:
If you run `az acr credential show` on an ACR instance that doesn't have the admin account enabled, it displays the following message:

```
Operation failed with status: 'Bad Request'. Details: Cannot perform credential operations for <RESOURCE_ID> as Admin user is disabled. Enable Admin user feature and try again.
```

The message definitely makes it clear what the issue is, but the whole `Operation failed with status: 'Bad Request'` prefix just makes it feel a little unpolished, as compared to other scenarios in the CLI.

For example, trying to run `az acr create` with a name that is already taken displays `The registry foobarbaz is already in use.`, which is a short and immediately understandable error.

issue_title: btTriangleInfoMap deserialization issue
issue_body: I've been trying to serialize/deserializing btTriangleInfoMap to avoid having to call btGenerateInternalEdgeInfo at runtime, but its effects (eliminating internal edge collisions) don't seem to survive the serialization/deserialization process. I looked into it and found that, when btAdjustInternalEdgeContacts() tries to look up a btTriangleInfo in the btTriangleInfoMap after the deserialization to do the contact adjustments, it fails because the btTriangleInfoMap claims that there is no entry for the given key. The cause of this seems to be that the capacity of m_valueArray is not maintained through the serialization/deserialization process, and because this value is used as part of the hash calculation the same keys will get different hash values before and after serialization/deserialization. I tried setting the capacity of m_keyArray and m_valueArray to the correct value (should be equal to the size of m_hashTable, right?) manually after the deserialization and that seemed to fix the problem. So I guess the solution would be to just add code in btTriangleInfoMap::deSerialize() that sets the capacity to the right value.

issue_title: graceful shutdown or reload with gunicorn without shutdown connections
issue_body:
How can I do a graceful shutdown or reload via gunicorn when there are pending connections(will be finished a little later), ensure the pending connections be finished, not directly shutdown by aiohttp?

```
# worker.py::GunicornWebWorker
    @asyncio.coroutine
    def close(self):
        if self.servers:
            servers = self.servers
            self.servers = None

            # stop accepting connections
            for server, handler in servers.items():
                self.log.info("Stopping server: %s, connections: %s",
                              self.pid, len(handler.connections))
                server.close()
                yield from server.wait_closed()

            # send on_shutdown event
            if hasattr(self.wsgi, 'shutdown'):
                yield from self.wsgi.shutdown()

            # stop alive connections
            tasks = [
                handler.shutdown(
                    timeout=self.cfg.graceful_timeout / 100 * 95)
                for handler in servers.values()]
            yield from asyncio.gather(*tasks, loop=self.loop)

            # cleanup application
            if hasattr(self.wsgi, 'cleanup'):
                yield from self.wsgi.cleanup()
```
when handler.connections > 0, i.e.,has pending connections, if the connection was just create, it will be shutdown throw:
    web_server.py::Server::shutdown -> web_protocol::RequestHandler::shutdown
    

```
# web_protocol::RequestHandler::shutdown
    @asyncio.coroutine
    def shutdown(self, timeout=15.0):
        """Worker process is about to exit, we need cleanup everything and
        stop accepting requests. It is especially important for keep-alive
        connections."""
        self._force_close = True
        # ...
```


here, by set self._force_close = True,
in data_received, the request will be ignored.


```
# web_protocol::RequestHandler::data_received
    @asyncio.coroutine
    def data_received(self, data):
        if self._force_close or self._close:
            return
        # ...
```


so, how to do a graceful shutdown or reload (fg, with gunicorn)?
a way of which is adding an on_shutdown handler, if one can check whether there are pending connections, he can wait until all connections finished with some seconds.
is this ok, or another better way?


My test code:


```
# server.py
from aiohttp import web 

async def handle(request):
    text = "Hello"
    return web.Response(text=text)

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_post('/{name:.*}', handle)
```


```
# client.py
import asyncio
import aiohttp
from aiohttp import web 


async def post():
    url = "http://127.0.0.1:8000/test"
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.post(url, data=b'test') as resp:
            pass


async def run():
    while 1:
        fus = [post() for i in range(20)]
        await asyncio.wait(fus)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(run())
```


```
# gunicorn.conf
bind = '0.0.0.0:8000'
backlog = 2048
workers = 1 
worker_class = 'aiohttp.GunicornWebWorker'
daemon = False
pidfile = 'pid.pid'
errorlog = 'error.log'
```

I can't find anything in the documentation that shows a way to do this. So I'm using the following approach:

```
app = web.Application()

As shown above.

CentOS / aiohttp 2.3 / Nginx

issue_title: Implement async context manager API for az.create
issue_body:
1) `az.create` should be coroutine if at some point we decide to include rabbit/kafka/other integration, it will be very essential. From other side the is breaking change, better to do this earlier.

```python
async with az.create(zipkin_address, endpoint, sample_rate=1.0) as tracer
    with tracer.new_trace(sampled=True) as span:
        span.name('root_span')

3)  Old approach should work also:
```python
tracer = await az.create(zipkin_address, endpoint, sample_rate=1.0)

issue_title: pointer being freed was not allocated
issue_body:
I added these 2 tiny little lines to a python script:

```
config.set_string('-agc', 'max')
config.set_string('-fillprob', '50')
```

Script was mostly working before but now it fails with:

```
Python(90397,0x7fff7d3af300) malloc: *** error for object 0x3e45798ee2308c3a: pointer being freed was not allocated
*** set a breakpoint in malloc_error_break to debug
```

Here is the full code listing:

```
#!/usr/local/bin/python

from pocketsphinx import *
import pyaudio

from os import environ, path
from itertools import izip
import sys

MODELDIR = "/usr/local//share/pocketsphinx/model/"

def print10best(decoder):
    # Access N best decodings.
    print 'Best 10 hypothesis: '
    for best, i in izip(decoder.nbest(), range(10)):
    	print best.hyp().best_score, best.hyp().hypstr

# Create a decoder with certain model
config = Decoder.default_config()
config.set_string('-hmm', path.join(MODELDIR, 'en-us/en-us'))
config.set_string('-lm', path.join(MODELDIR, 'en-us/en-us.lm.dmp'))
config.set_string('-dict', path.join(MODELDIR, 'en-us/cmudict-en-us.dict'))
config.set_string('-jsgf', './my.jsgf')
config.set_string('-dictcase', 'yes')
config.set_string('-agc', 'max')
config.set_string('-fillprob', '50')

decoder = Decoder(config)



p = pyaudio.PyAudio()

stream = p.open(format=pyaudio.paInt16, channels=1, rate=16000, input=True, frames_per_buffer=2048)
stream.start_stream()
in_speech_bf = True
decoder.start_utt()
while True:
    buf = stream.read(2048)
    if buf:
        decoder.process_raw(buf, False, False)
        try:
            if  decoder.hyp().hypstr != '':
                print 'Partial decoding result:', decoder.hyp().hypstr
                # print10best(decoder)
        except AttributeError:
            pass
        if decoder.get_in_speech():
            sys.stdout.write('.')
            sys.stdout.flush()
        if decoder.get_in_speech() != in_speech_bf:
            in_speech_bf = decoder.get_in_speech()
            if not in_speech_bf:
                decoder.end_utt()
                try:
                    if  decoder.hyp().hypstr != '':
                        print 'Stream decoding result:', decoder.hyp().hypstr
                except AttributeError:
                    pass
                decoder.start_utt()
    else:
        break
decoder.end_utt()
print 'An Error occured:', decoder.hyp().hypstr
```

issue_title: How can I wrap a function that I want to differentiate with autograd?

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import jacobian

th = np.array([1., 2., 3., 4.])
A = lambda th: [ [th[0], th[1]], [th[2], th[3]] ]
B = lambda th: np.array(A(th), np.float64)
jacobian(B)(th)
```
But this does not:
```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import jacobian

th = np.array([1., 2., 3., 4])
A = lambda th: [ [th[0], th[1]], [th[2], th[3]] ]
B = lambda f: lambda th: np.array(f(th), np.float64)
C = B(A)
jacobian(C)(th)
```
throwing `AutogradHint: This error *might* be caused by assigning into arrays, which autograd doesn't support.`

issue_title: Add post-sync build directive for modules
issue_body:
Currently if a module's source requires a build process to compile after syncing that's more involved than picking or dropping certain files, said process needs to be run after a `peru sync`. 

The feature would be specifying in `peru.yaml` a script within the module to execute after a `peru sync`. Perhaps use keyword `build`.

It's possible to script this in a watcher that wraps `peru` sync operations, so maybe this is out of scope for the project, similar to `peru override watch` #163. (?)

issue_title: Records with no ID parsed incorrectly
issue_body:
Given the following record:

> @misc{
author = {AMAP},
title = {Summary – The Greenland Ice Sheet in a Changing Climate: Snow, Water, Ice and Permafrost in the Arctic (SWIPA)},
publisher = {Arctic Monitoring and Assessment Programme (AMAP)},
pages = {22 pp.},
year = {2009},
type = {Generic},
}

The parser sets ID as "author = {AMAP" and doesn't recognize the author field.

Changing `re.split(',\s*\n|\n\s*,', record)` to `re.split('(?<={)\s*\n|,\s*\n|\n\s*,', record)` line 239 of bparser.py seems to fix the problem.

issue_title: Decouple Reactive Bus from Hooks
issue_body:
The core idea of the reactive framework is that handlers should be triggered by changes to flags (erstwhile reactive states).  It shouldn’t matter what causes those flags to change: it could be due to a hook, an action, or some application event entirely outside of Juju for cloud-native type applications.  Regardless, when the flags that a given handler cares about are changed and match that handlers conditions, that handler should be invoked.

Due to implementation details, however, the bus is currently tightly coupled to hooks.  This leads to many issues and inconsistencies, such as handlers being reinvoked even though the relevant flags haven't changed, flags set by the application or actions not triggering handlers immediately, the dichotomy between `@hook` and flags, etc.

Proposal
------------

We should have a proper, independent queue of pending handlers.  Whenever a flag is changed, registered handlers that "care" about that flag should be tested and, if matched, added to the handler queue.

There are two possible approaches for processing the handler queue.  There could be a persistent reactive bus service that watches the queue and uses `juju-run` to invoke the handlers.  It is necessary to use `juju-run` because handlers need a Juju context to use the Juju hook tools, such as `config-get` and `relation-get` and the bus service would inherently be outside of a Juju context.

Alternatively, a consumer could be spawned on-demand when something is added to the queue, either as a separate thread or process if a Juju context is currently active, or via `juju-run` if the queue is updated outside of a Juju context.  This approach would be more complex, but would save some latency and overhead in the common case where a Juju context is already available.  In particular, the process spawning the consumer in a Juju context would need to ensure it doesn't exit until the worker is complete to ensure that the Juju context remains viable.  (This could perhaps be accomplished with an `atexit` handler.)

Either way, the code modifying the flags should be able to expect the handlers to be invoked automatically.

issue_title: Need upgrade path from non-reactive charms to reactive
issue_body: Doing in-place upgrades from a non-reactive charm to a reactive implementation is difficult primarily due to needing to get the reactive states set up to reflect the environment as is currently deployed.  Part of this can be done in an `@hook('upgrade-charm')` handler, but we at a minimum need some sort of helper to get the interface layers to the correct state.

issue_title: Removing an agent interferes with RandomActivation step method
issue_body:
First of all, thanks so much for all the great work y'all've put into this. It's let me explore agent based modeling very quickly and easily.

I am implementing a model where agents have a finite lifespan, however, I noticed that my agents were persisting in the model (their AgentIDs were present in more Steps) than they should be for their lifespan. I tracked this down to a conflict between removing an agent and stepping through the model. If the agent's `step()` removes the agent from the model, this modifies the list of agents being iterated over in the scheduler's `step()` (starting at line 63 in [schedule.py](https://github.com/projectmesa/mesa/blob/master/examples/WolfSheep/wolf_sheep/schedule.py); `step_breed()` is called by `step()`):

```python
def step_breed(self, breed):
    '''
    Shuffle order and run all agents of a given breed.
    Args:
        breed: Class object of the breed to run.
    '''
    agents = self.agents_by_breed[breed]
    random.shuffle(agents)
    for agent in agents: # <- If agent is removed, 'agents' is modified
        agent.step()
```

This leads to undesirable behavior (at least in the RandomActivation scheduler) where not all agents are called in each iteration if an agent is removed from the list. In the code below, all the agents should be present for only two steps maximum, however, some are present for more than that (the value varies by run presumably because of the shuffling of agents in the scheduler). Here's an example of what I mean:

```python
from mesa.time import RandomActivation
from mesa.datacollection import DataCollector
from mesa import *
import numpy as np

class TestModel(Model):
    def __init__(self, agent_lifetime = 1, n_agents = 100):
        self.agent_lifetime = agent_lifetime
        self.n_agents = n_agents

        ## keep track of the the remaining life of an agent and
        ## how many ticks it has seen
        self.datacollector = DataCollector(
            agent_reporters = {"remaining_life" : lambda a: a.remaining_life,
                               "steps" : lambda a: a.steps})
        
        self.current_ID = 0
        self.schedule = RandomActivation(self)
        
        for _ in range(n_agents):
            self.schedule.add(FiniteLifeAgent(self.get_next_ID(),
                                              self.agent_lifetime))
            
    def get_next_ID(self):
        self.current_ID += 1
        return self.current_ID

    def step(self):
        '''Add agents back to n_agents in each step'''
        self.schedule.step()
        self.datacollector.collect(self)

        if len(self.schedule.agents) < self.n_agents:
            for _ in range(self.n_agents - len(self.schedule.agents)):
                self.schedule.add(FiniteLifeAgent(self.get_next_ID(),
                                                  self.agent_lifetime))

    def run_model(self, step_count = 100):
        for _ in range(step_count):
            self.step()

class FiniteLifeAgent(Agent):
    
    '''
    An agent that is supposed to live for a finite number of ticks.
    Also has a 10% chance of dying in each tick.
    '''
    
    def __init__(self, unique_id, lifetime):
        self.unique_id = unique_id
        self.remaining_life = lifetime
        self.steps = 0

    def step(self, model):
        inactivated = self.inactivate(model)
        self.steps += 1 # keep track of how many ticks are seen
        if np.random.binomial(1,0.1) is not 0: # 10% chance of dying
            model.schedule.remove(self)
            
    def inactivate(self, model):
        self.remaining_life -= 1
        if self.remaining_life < 0:
            model.schedule.remove(self)
            return True
        return False


model = TestModel()
model.run_model()

df = model.datacollector.get_agent_vars_dataframe()
df = df.reset_index()

# Get the total number of ticks an agent is present in. 
lifetimes = df.groupby(["AgentID"]).Step.agg({"Step" : lambda x: len(x)})
# Varies between ~8 and 14 depending on the run.
print("Total number of ticks an agent is alive in should be 2. Actual value is: %i" % lifetimes.Step.max())

# actual value is 1 and 0, respectively, showing that all agents aren't called in each step of the scheduler
print("Total steps seen by agent should be 1. Actual value is: %i" % df.steps.max())
print("Minimum remaining life of agent should be 0. Actual value is: %i" % df.remaining_life.min())
```

I have fixed this for my model by having the scheduler keep track of a dictionary of agents keyed by a unique integer that I stored in the agent's `unique_id` slot rather than a list of agents:

```python
class BaseScheduler:
    """ Simplest scheduler; activates agents one at a time, in the order
    they were added.

    Assumes that each agent added has a *step* method which takes no arguments.

    (This is explicitly meant to replicate the scheduler in MASON).

    """
    model = None
    steps = 0
    time = 0
    agents = {}

    def __init__(self, model):
        """ Create a new, empty BaseScheduler. """
        self.model = model
        self.steps = 0
        self.time = 0
        self.agents = {}

    def add(self, agent):
        """ Add an Agent object to the schedule.

        Args:
            agent: An Agent to be added to the schedule. NOTE: The agent must
            have a step() method.

        """
        self.agents[agent.unique_id] = agent

    def remove(self, agent):
        """ Remove all instances of a given agent from the schedule.

        Args:
            agent: An agent object.

        """
        del self.agents[agent.unique_id]

    def step(self):
        """ Execute the step of all the agents, one at a time. """
        agent_keys = list(self.agents.keys())
        for agent_key in agent_keys:
            self.agents[agent_key].step(self.model)
        self.steps += 1
        self.time += 1

    def get_agent_count(self):
        """ Returns the current number of agents in the queue. """
        return len(self.agents.keys())
```

By iterating over the dictionary keys rather than the agents themselves, if an agent is removed the other agents are still stepped by the scheduler correctly. 

I've made changes to the relevant classes in `time.py` in order to implement this, and they pass the unit tests. However, all classes that inherit the BaseScheduler class in example models would need to be examined and probably fixed. I also noticed that in some example models the `unique_id` slot is being used for other purposes than just identifying the agent. For example, in the WolfSheep model, it's being used to store the position of the agent. There could be other examples that would need updated, too.

I would be happy to fix up the WolfSheep model and submit a pull request if that would be helpful, but please advise this total newcomer to collaborative software how best to proceed!

best,
Colin

issue_title: Update License to Apache & push change.
LiveServer is not terminated cleanly which breaks pytest-cov functionality
issue_body:
When using the `live_server` with `pytest-cov`, the coverage is not reported correctly. As mentioned in the issues pytest-dev/pytest-cov#44 and jarus/flask-testing#70, the problem is that the `live_server` process is not being terminated cleanly.

One of the proposed solutions in the flask-testing project works well here too:

```
def _clean_liveserver_stop(self):
    """Stop application process."""
    if self._process:
        try:
            os.kill(self._process.pid, signal.SIGINT)
            self._process.join(LIVESERVER_TIMEOUT)
        except Exception as ex:
            logging.error('Failed to join the live server process: %r', ex)
        finally:
            if self._process.is_alive():
                # If it's still alive, kill it
                self._process.terminate()
```

The problem is that such a solution is not cross-platform. How about adding a config option that enables/disables cleanly terminating the live server? Or always trying to cleanly exit the live server process and warn on platforms which do not support that easily/directly/without too much work?

I can submit a pull request if needed.

issue_title: Avoid spawning worker node processes unnecessarily
issue_body:
Hi! I have configured my project to automatically set a value of `-n` based on the number of available CPUs in the host where the tests are being run. Also, the SUT is started with a level of resources proportional to the value of `-n` so it can handle the load properly. The higher the number of resources required by the SUT the longer it takes to start (and the higher the monetary cost is, since these resources are AWS stuff).
This usually pays off really well, and thanks to xdist I've managed to reduce considerably the time it takes to run the whole test suite.

However when somebody wants to run a subset of tests, based on filters like `-k foo`, even if only 2 or 3 tests match the filter, xdist will still spawn all of the `-n` processes unnecessarily. And it will usually take longer to run with xdist than without (because of the SUT startup time)

I tried to find, with no luck, an appropriate pytest hook that kicks in after the tests have been collected but before xdist creates the worker nodes, so I can set `numprocesses = min(numprocesses, tests_collected)` and set up the SUT with the appropriate amount of resources. But it seems all the tests are collected after the worker nodes are spawned (and they are collected in every single node?).

I hope I made myself clear. Any clues on how can this be solved?

Thanks!

issue_title: renewal instructions need help
issue_body:
#### Observed text:
At https://letsencrypt.org/howitworks/#renewing-a-certificate it says:
> To renew a certificate, simply run letsencrypt again providing the same values when prompted. In almost all circumstances, renewal should be performed with the certonly subcommand

**BUG:** In that passage, the second sentence contradicts the first.

Also it is not nearly as detailed as it should be.

#### Suggested improved text:

Except as noted below, the process of renewing a certificate is closely parallel to the process you used for generating the certificate initially.  Some exceptions and related observations are:
* The main exception is that if you initially used letsencrypt in **run** mode (which is the default), you should use **certonly** mode for the renewal.  This will add the new certificate into the /etc/letsencrypt/archive/$NAME/ directory, and update the symlinks in /etc/letsencrypt/live/$NAME/.
  * The web server (and anything else that references only the symlinks) should work without further effort.
  * On the other hand, any postprocessing or customization you performed initially may have to be repeated. 
    *  For example, if you added custom DH parameters to the end of the cert.pem file, you will have to repeat this.
    * Similarly, if you copied the *.pem files somewhere else so they could be used by your SMTP server (not just your web server), you will have to repeat this.
* If you initially used the **certonly** mode, you should use **certonly** mode for the renewal.
  * Any postprocessing or customization may have to be repeated, as discussed above.
* If your certificate covers multiple domain names, bear in mind that the certificate files are named after the first domain mentioned on the command line, as discussed in item #1376.  Therefore you should make sure the renewal mentions the same domain first;  otherwise letsencrypt will think you are asking for a new, unrelated certificate.
* It is possible to renew the certificate using different parameters on the command line.  If the differences are not too extreme, the new certificate will function as a replacement for the old.
  * For example, if your server has multiple domain names, you can mention more or fewer aliases in the renewal.  Letsencrypt will warn about this (unless you specify the --expand option).
  * If you don't remember what domain names you specified initially, you can figure it out by examining the old certificate.
    *  One way to do this is by using a browser.  Visit your site, then click on the :lock: padlock icon next to the URL.
    * Another way is to use a command like this:
```
:;  :| openssl s_client -CApath /etc/ssl/certs -connect $TARGET:443 \
        | openssl x509 -text -noout  |& egrep -C1 'Subject:|Name:|After.:'
```
* If you have multiple certificates with the same first name, the renewal process is ambiguous.  The best way to deal with this is ???????????? (FIXME).

Description
===========
There are lots of cases where "on Avro schema per topic" does not work. A good description of the cases and solutions available in Java library is provided here:
https://www.confluent.io/blog/put-several-event-types-kafka-topic/

However, the Python library has "subject = topic + key/value" hardcoded, and even the parameter name in `encode_record_with_schema` is called `topic`, not `subject`.

While this behavior could be overriden in subclasses, would be great to have "NameStrategies" compatible/similar to java client implementation.

issue_title: Manual commit not working properly
issue_body:
Checklist
===========
confluent-kafka-python : ('0.11.0', '720896')
librdkafka: ('0.9.4-134-g75013b', 591104)
Kafka broker version: 0.11.0.0 running on Docker (confluentinc/cp-kafka:latest)
OS: Arch Linux
No exceptions in the Kafka log.

The provided logs (cgrp,topic,protocol,broker) includes the scenario described in the "How to reproduce" section.
[consumer.log.gz](https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/files/1421491/consumer.log.gz)

Description
===========
Manually committing the consumer offsets more then once hangs the consumer process and the CPU usage becomes very high.

I've nailed down to where the problem was introduced and it seems like it stopped working after this commit: https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/commit/6ef1ae40efc5f044bf497d1cb4903d0fb968edcf

How to reproduce
================
1. Install latest version of confluent-kafka-python (commit 7ebc807).
```sh
pip install -U git+git://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python.git@master
```
2. Start a consumer with auto commit disabled.
```python
from confluent_kafka import Consumer

config = {
    'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092',
    'group.id': 'foobar.group',
    'enable.auto.commit': False
}
consumer = Consumer(**config)
consumer.subscribe(['foobar'])

try:
    while True:
        msg = consumer.poll(timeout=0.1)
        if msg is None:
            continue
        if msg.error():
            continue
        message = msg.value().decode('utf-8')
        print(message)
        print("Committing consumer offsets")
        consumer.commit()
finally:
    consumer.close()
```
3. Produce a message
```sh
echo $(date) | kafkacat -P -b localhost -t foobar
```
4. The first message is consumed and committed correctly, but if you produce another message the consumer process will hung on the `poll` method and the CPU usage becomes very high. If you restart the consumer process it will catch up on the previous message because the offset was never committed, but as soon as you produce another message the same problem occurs.

Checklist
=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [x] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`):
 - [x] Apache Kafka broker version:
 - [x] Client configuration: `{...}`
 - [x] Operating system:
 - [x] Provide client logs (with `'debug': '..'` as necessary)
 - [x] Provide broker log excerpts
 - [x] Critical issue

issue_title: Please add full key fingerprint to the README file
issue_body:
In the README.rst file there is a reference to the long-id of the gpg key used to sign the releases which is incorrectly identified as a "fingerprint".

While long id collisions aren't currently as trivial as short id ones, good practice is to use the full fingerprint for key verification, so it would be useful to have it in the readme.

At the very least, an `s/Signing key fingerprint/Signing key ID/` would be required to make the README formally correct.

Thanks in advance

issue_title: Blob does not exist in Postgres
issue_body:
I've got the `AlchemySessionContainer` setup with my Postgres connection on heroku, but if I then try to use it I get this error:
```
(psycopg2.ProgrammingError) type "blob" does not exist
LINE 7:  auth_key BLOB, 
                  ^
 [SQL: '
CREATE TABLE telethonsessions (
	session_id VARCHAR NOT NULL, 
	dc_id INTEGER NOT NULL, 
	server_address VARCHAR, 
	port INTEGER, 
	auth_key BLOB, 
	PRIMARY KEY (session_id, dc_id)
)

']
```

It seems to be the case that the BLOB type does not exist in Postgres (there they call it a BYTEA). The solution would seem to be to replace that with the [LargeBinary](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/type_basics.html#sqlalchemy.types.LargeBinary) type which should resolve to the correct type per database.

issue_body:
Using cpython 2.7

`test.py`:
```
def foo():
    a = {
        i: i**i
        for i in range(10000)}
    print "END"

foo()
```
```
$ python -m vmprof --lines -o test.prof test.py
END
$ vmprofshow --lines test.prof
Total hits: 1480 s
File: test.py
Function: <dictcomp> at line 3
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/vmprofshow", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/vmprof/show.py", line 159, in main
    pp.show(args.profile)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/vmprof/show.py", line 182, in show
    self.show_func(filename, funline, funname, line_stats)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/vmprof/show.py", line 234, in show_func
    sublines = inspect.getblock(all_lines[start_lineno-1:])
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/inspect.py", line 677, in getblock
    tokenize.tokenize(iter(lines).next, blockfinder.tokeneater)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/tokenize.py", line 169, in tokenize
    tokenize_loop(readline, tokeneater)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/tokenize.py", line 175, in tokenize_loop
    for token_info in generate_tokens(readline):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/tokenize.py", line 357, in generate_tokens
    raise TokenError, ("EOF in multi-line statement", (lnum, 0))
tokenize.TokenError: ('EOF in multi-line statement', (6, 0))
```

Specifically the line `        i: i**i` takes the most time. But when handling a tokenize error the same way as a file missing I get:
```
Total hits: 1480 s
File: test.py
Function: <dictcomp> at line 3

Line #     Hits   % Hits  Line Contents
=======================================
     3        1      0.1
     4     1479     99.9

=======================================

     2                        a = {
     3                            i: i**i
     4                            for i in range(10000)}
     5                        print "END"

Total hits: 2 s

I tried playing around with `inspect.getblock` for dictionary comprehension by hand and was unable to get it to work properly.

So I am not sure that multiline dictionary comprehensions are measured correctly per line.

At the very least we can prevent `vmprofshow --lines test.prof` from crashing by handling the tokenize error in `vmprof.show`.

issue_title: Remove MarshallingError and UnmarshallingError in favor of a single ValidationError
issue_body:
Currently, `MarshallingError` and `UnmarshallingError` signal that an error should be stored in the `errors` dictionary during marshalling and unmarshalling. 

These exceptions only served a purpose prior to commit https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/commit/00a18667b5a7a6a630d07062693e50007eefa5c8, when all exceptions were caught and reraised as (Un)MarshallingErrors. Now, they are only raised when a validation error occurs.

I propose to remove these exception classes in favor of a single `ValidationError`. Doing so would reduce the API and make accessing error messages when using `strict` mode much cleaner.

**Current**:

```python
from marshmallow.exceptions import MarshallingError, UnmarshallingError, ValidationError

schema = MySchema(strict=True)

try:
    schema.dump(some_obj)
except MarshallingError as err:
    if isinstance(err.underlying_error, ValidationError):
        messages = err.underlying_error.messages
    # ...

try:
    schema.load(some_obj)
except UnmarshallingError as err:
    if isinstance(err.underlying_error, ValidationError):
        messages = err.underlying_error.messages
    # ...
```

**Proposed**:

```python
from marshmallow.exceptions import ValidationError

schema = MySchema(strict=True)

try:
    schema.dump(some_obj)
except ValidationError as err:
    messages = err.messages
    # ...

try:
    schema.load(some_obj)
except ValidationError as err:
    messages = err.messages
    # ...
```

This could be implemented in conjunction with https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/128 .

**WARNING**: This would be a major breaking change for people using `strict` mode.

issue_title: Clarification: Providing a custom dict class to Schema
issue_body:
Just wanting to see if I'm understanding this correctly: If I need to provide a custom mapping class to put the serialized data into, I should override the `dict_class` property on Schema. 

A simple example: Using Marshmallow to serialize response for display in a View.

```python
class DottedDict(dict):
    "Preserve dotted access to pretend we're accessing the original object"
    __getattr__ = dict.__getitem__


# use this class as the base for all my schema
class ViewModelSchema(Schema):
    dict_class = DottedDict

# when dump is called, result.data will be a DottedDict
class UserSchema(ViewModelSchema):
    class Meta:
        fields = ['username', 'email', 'registered_at']
```

Is this considered a "public API" or am I backdooring. I understand that setting `ordered` wouldn't affect anything now -- but that's not a concern if I'm simply serializing data down to use in a template instead of shoving it off into a JSON API.

I'd hope that I could provide the `dict_class` keyword directly to dump, but since it already provides the argument to the Marshaller, a TypeError multiple values for dict_class pops out.

If this is a public API, would a PR for documentation on this be accepted?

issue_title: Schema.dump(None) Should Follow JSONAPI Spec
issue_body:
According to the [JSONAPI Spec](http://jsonapi.org/format/#document-top-level):
> Primary data MUST be either... a single resource object, a single resource identifier object, or null, for requests that target single resources
and when [fetching empty relationships](http://jsonapi.org/format/#fetching-relationships-responses-200) you must return a 200 with a `null` or `[]` for the data key.

In this example we don't properly follow the spec:
```python
>>> from marshmallow_jsonapi import Schema, fields
>>> class PostSchema(Schema):
...     id = fields.Str(dump_only=True)
...     class Meta:
...         type_ = 'posts'
...         strict = True
... 
>>> schema = PostSchema()
>>> schema.dump(None).data
{'data': {'type': 'posts'}}
>>> schema.dump([]).data
{'data': {'type': 'posts'}}
```

Further there is an issue with self links and None or [] objects:
```python
>>> class PostSchema(Schema):
...     id = fields.Str(dump_only=True)
...     class Meta:
...         type_ = 'posts'
...         strict = True
...         self_url_kwargs = {'id': '<id>'}
...         self_url = '/articles/{id}'
... 
>>> schema = PostSchema()
>>> schema.dump(None).data
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "./marshmallow/schema.py", line 515, in dump
    original_data=obj)
  File "./marshmallow/schema.py", line 830, in _invoke_dump_processors
    data=data, many=many, original_data=original_data)
  File "./marshmallow/schema.py", line 930, in _invoke_processors
    data = utils.if_none(processor(data, many), data)
  File "./marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py", line 113, in format_json_api_response
    ret = self.format_items(data, many)
  File "./marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py", line 276, in format_items
    return self.format_item(data)
  File "./marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py", line 263, in format_item
    links = self.get_resource_links(item)
  File "./marshmallow_jsonapi/schema.py", line 294, in get_resource_links
    kwargs = resolve_params(item, self.opts.self_url_kwargs or {})
  File "./marshmallow_jsonapi/utils.py", line 33, in resolve_params
    'attribute of {obj!r}'.format(attr_name=attr_name, obj=obj)
AttributeError: 'id' is not a valid attribute of {}
```
(Same is true for `[]`)

I'm working on a fork and a fix for this currently.

issue_title: document API difference between 0.1 and 0.2
issue_body:
constructor API difference between 0.1 and 0.2
- LSTM - 0.2 added `p, wRegularizer, uRegularizer, bRegularizer`
- BatchNormalization - 0.2 added `initWeight, initBias, initGradWeight, initGradBias`, removed `initMethod`
- GRU - 0.2 added `p, wRegularizer, uRegularizer, bRegularizer`, removed `initMethod`
- RNN - 0.2 added `wRegularizer, uRegularizer, bRegularizer`, remove  `initMethod`
- SpatialDilatedConvolution - 0.2 added `wRegularizer, bRegularizer`, removed `initmethod`
- SpatialConvolutionMap - 0.2 added `wRegularizer, bRegularizer`
-  Linear - 0.2 added `wRegularizer, bRegularizer, initWeight, initBias, initGradWeight, initGradBias`, removed `initMethod`
- CAddTable - 0.2 added `inplace`
- LookupTable - 0.2 added `wRegularizer`
- LSTMPeephole - 0.2 added `p, wRegularizer, uRegularizer, bRegularizer`


Layer newly added in 0.2, not exist in 0.1 
- VolumetricMaxPooling - not exist in 0.1
- Graph - not exist in 0.1
- Input - not exist in 0.1
- Pack - not exist in 0.1

issue_title: use development branches for larger features and changes
issue_body:
Is the pvlib community interested in using development branches for larger features and changes? The idea is to get relatively large proposals in the hands of more people before they're merged. For example, #124 is a large feature addition and #151 breaks the existing API, so they require a 0.x version change to merge instead of only a 0.x.y change. More importantly, I am reluctant to merge large changes without more significant user feedback. 

But critical user feedback is harder to get if it requires navigating the git maze. **If** you're comfortable using git then it's very easy (and powerful) to checkout another user's PR to try for yourself, but if you're newish to git and using GitHub Desktop or similar then it's somewhere between a mystery and impossible to do that. I think (hope?) that newish users of git and GitHub Desktop can switch branches of a single remote repository without too much trouble.

How would this work in practice? All features would still start out as PRs against master. Depending on the scope, we may decide to add it as a new branch once the code is useable. One of the maintainers would have to complete that step. The original PR can then be closed, and a new PR can be opened from the development branch to the master branch. The original developer can still submit his/her own PRs against the development branch. The maintainers can quickly merge these PRs with abandon since they don't impact master. Once everything is good, the development branch can be merged into master and deleted. 

Either way, I'm planning to push a development branch for the forecasting/expanded-io-module PR tomorrow because I think it will make accessing the code/documentation for my PVSC paper/poster slightly less confusing.

Thoughts?

@jforbess and @cwhanse might have some perspective on this.
